old_claim.note: Naval Logbooks provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: The Spanish system applied a merchant-convoy pattern to colonial treasure routes, grouping merchant ships in scheduled fleets while adding state-mandated armed escort.

source_shape.source_locator: The NPS lesson says Spain required merchant ships to sail in fleets or convoys escorted by armed warships for protection, and that the fleet system began in the 1530s.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source defines the Spanish fleet system as merchant ships grouped into fleets or convoys with armed warship escort.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: That makes the merchant-convoy organization the shared commercial-fleet pattern. Naval logbooks may document voyages, but the cited fleet-system source does not make logbooks a direct prerequisite.

ontology_before: Naval Convoy Systems depended on naval_logbooks, tying the convoy concept to voyage recordkeeping rather than to grouped merchant shipping.
ontology_after: Spanish Treasure Fleet Convoys depend contextually on the merchant convoy pattern while adding state escort and imperial treasure-route organization.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: naval_convoy_systems now depends on merchant_convoy_systems and no longer directly depends on naval_logbooks.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to naval recordkeeping rather than Spanish treasure-fleet convoys.
A source showed logbook standardization was required before the Spanish fleet system could operate.
The merchant_convoy_systems node were later narrowed to exclude maritime merchant fleets.
short_reason: The source-backed system is merchant ships grouped into protected convoys, not logbook practice.
